name: Wen Qin (Zhongruo)

sections.biography-source-undefined:
Wen Qin, styled Zhongruo, was from Qiao commandery. It is suggested he may be from Qiao county. He was a son of Wen Ji, a minor general.

It is said that Wen Qin communicated with Wei Feng in 219, but it is unclear whether he was aware of his plot to overthrow Cao Cao. Still, Wen Qin was imprisoned and beat one hundred times before being spared from death by Cao Cao because of Wen Ji (whether it was his pleads or his achievements).

The Book of Wei describes Wen Qin as “strict, violent, and rude. Where he was, his arrogance and hubris made him place himself above others. He did not heed the laws of his post.” For this, Cao Rui tried to curb him whenever he petitioned for himself to be sent somewhere.

Wen Qin was made Grand Administrator of Lujiang. Wang Ling claimed in a memorial that Wen Qin was greedy for money and that he should not be trusted with a border commandery. He suggested removing and punishing Wen Qin from his office, but Wen Qin only got removed and summoned back.

When Cao Shuang was wielding power during the Zhengshi era (240–249, he treated Wen Qin well as they were from the same county, and assigned him back to his former post in Lujiang with additional honors. There, Wen Qin became more overbearing and haughty. He became notorious for boasting about his importance as well as being condescending to others since he was braver than them.

In 249, Cao Shuang was killed. The new government led by Sima Yi assigned Wen Qin to be General of the Front to try and calm him down, but this did not assuage his fears. Wen Qin was made Inspector of Yang province after Zhuge Dan left the post. Wen Qin, after joining with Guanqiu Jian, rebelled against Wei in 255, claiming that the authority of Sima Shi (who succeeded Sima Yi by this time) was damaging to the state. However, they were defeated and Guanqiu Jian killed, but Wen Qin managed to escape to Wu.

In Wu, he constantly lobbied the imperial government to invade Wei, and was well-received. He was stationed around the northern border by the powerful official, Sun Jun. He came in minor conflict with the other Wu generals there, Lü Fan and Zhu Yi, because of his refusal to obey them. Despite this, Sun Jun heavily trusted Wen Qin and often had him in his entourage during his campaign against Wei.

In 256, he was sent as one of many generals to put down the perceived rebellion of Lü Fan against Sun Chen, and succeeded. In the 6th lunar month of 257, he was sent to go aid Zhuge Dan in Shouchun. Although Wen Qin initially worked together with Zhuge Dan, policy disagreements turned violent and Zhuge Dan killed Wen Qin. Wen Hu and Wen Yang, the two sons of Wen Qin, surrendered to Sima Zhao. They helped demoralize the defenders and the escaping Zhuge Dan was killed by the Wei forces, avenging the death of their father.

His clan was eventually exterminated for being accused of supposedly associating with the executed despised regent Yang Jun by Sima Yao, a maternal grandson of Zhuge Dan.

full_text:
Wen Qin (Zhongruo) 文欽 (仲若)
ARomance of the Three KingdomsEncyclopedia officer profile.
Wen Qin (文欽), styledZhongruo (仲若). Inspector of Yang. Rebelled against Sima Shi with Guanqiu Jian. Wen Qin was affiliated with the Wei Kingdom and the Wu Kingdom. Wen Qin lived during the Three Kingdoms periods of Chinese history.

The Book of Weidescribes Wen Qin as “strict, violent, and rude. Where he was, his arrogance and hubris made him place himself above others. He did not heed the laws of his post.” For this, Cao Rui tried to curb him whenever he petitioned for himself to be sent somewhere.
